title: Container Networking
description:
## How Containers Talk

By default Docker attaches every container to the built-in **`bridge`** network. Containers on it can reach the outside world, but they can only reach *each other* by IP address — **not by name**.

### User-defined bridge networks

Create your own network and Docker adds an **embedded DNS server**: containers on the same user-defined network can resolve each other by **container name**.

```bash
docker network create appnet
docker run -d --name db    --network appnet postgres:alpine
docker run -d --name web   --network appnet nginx:alpine
# 'web' can now reach the database at the hostname 'db'
```

### Network types you'll meet

| Driver | Use |
|---|---|
| `bridge` | Default; isolated network on a single host (user-defined bridges add name resolution). |
| `host` | Container shares the host's network stack directly (no isolation, no port mapping). |
| `none` | No networking at all. |

### Publishing vs connecting

- **Publishing** (`-p 8080:80`) exposes a container port to the **host**.
- **Connecting to a network** (`--network appnet`) lets containers talk to **each other**.

These are independent: you publish to reach a service from outside, and you share a network to wire services together internally.

Mark complete, then build a two-container network in the next task.
